article_title: Your old router is the secret to a stable smart home
core_thesis: Repurposing an old router as a dedicated subnetwork for IoT devices solves Wi-Fi congestion and security issues in modern smart homes, where 30+ devices often overwhelm consumer routers. By offloading smart home traffic to a separate 2.4 GHz network and creating network segmentation, users can improve performance for high-speed devices and isolate vulnerable IoT gadgets from critical data.
detailed_summary: The article explains that modern smart homes often have 30–100+ Wi-Fi devices (bulbs, plugs, cameras), causing consumer routers to choke and drop connections. The solution is to use an old router as a dedicated IoT subnetwork. Benefits include: (1) Security – by connecting the old router's WAN port to the main router's LAN port, a firewall is created that traps compromised IoT devices on the secondary network, protecting PCs, NAS, and banking data. (2) Performance – moving all 2.4 GHz IoT devices to the old router frees up the main router's 5/6 GHz bands for high-speed tasks like streaming and gaming. The setup process involves a factory reset, changing the old router's LAN IP to be within the main network's range but outside its DHCP pool, disabling DHCP on the old router, and then connecting via LAN-to-LAN (for a switch/AP) or LAN-to-WAN (for a shielded subnet). The article also suggests using the old router as an access point to extend Wi-Fi to dead zones. It concludes that this approach is a cost-effective alternative to expensive mesh systems.

entities.name: Jasmine Mannan
entities.role: Author of the article, Software and PC Hardware Author at XDA.

key_data_points: Modern smart homes often have 30–100+ Wi-Fi devices, and most consumer routers start to fail around 30 devices (🟢 relevant to your homelab: understanding device limits helps in designing robust networks).

Using an old router as a dedicated IoT subnetwork creates network segmentation: connecting its WAN port to the main router's LAN port forms a firewall that isolates compromised IoT devices from the main network (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: this segmentation is a defensive technique you can test or bypass in engagements).

Offloading 2.4 GHz IoT devices to the old router clears congestion on the main router's 5/6 GHz bands, improving performance for high-speed devices (🟢 relevant to your homelab: practical performance tuning for multi-device environments).

The setup requires: factory reset (30-second hold method), changing the old router's LAN IP to a static address inside the main network's range but outside its DHCP pool, and disabling DHCP on the old router to avoid IP conflicts (🟢 relevant to your DevOps/homelab: precise network configuration steps).

Two connection modes: LAN-to-LAN makes the old router a switch/AP (fastest, stable); LAN-to-WAN creates a shielded subnet with a firewall (🟢 relevant to Red Teaming: understanding network segmentation for lateral movement assessments).
methodology_steps: 1. Factory reset the old router: hold the pinhole reset button for 30 seconds, then continue holding while plugging in power for another 30 seconds, then hold for a final 30 seconds after power is fully on. This ensures a deep wipe.

2. Log into the old router via a laptop connected with an Ethernet cable. Change its LAN IP to a static address within your main network's subnet but outside the main router's DHCP pool (e.g., if main router is 192.168.1.1 with DHCP range 192.168.1.100-200, set old router to 192.168.1.2).
3. Disable the DHCP server on the old router (found in LAN settings). Only one DHCP server should exist on the network.

4. Choose connection mode: For a switch/AP (bridge), connect an Ethernet cable from a LAN port on the main router to a LAN port on the old router. For a shielded subnet, connect a LAN port on the main router to the WAN port on the old router.
5. (Optional) To use as an access point for dead zones, place the old router in the desired location, connect via Ethernet to the main router, and configure it in access point mode.
conclusions: Repurposing an old router as a dedicated IoT subnetwork is a cost-effective and practical solution to Wi-Fi congestion and security risks in smart homes, eliminating the need for expensive mesh systems. The article provides clear, step-by-step instructions for setup, emphasizing the importance of disabling DHCP and choosing the correct connection mode.

Network segmentation via a second router significantly enhances security by isolating vulnerable IoT devices, preventing compromised devices from accessing sensitive data on the main network. This is a simple but effective defense-in-depth measure.

The approach also improves performance by dedicating the 2.4 GHz band to IoT devices and freeing up higher bands for bandwidth-intensive tasks, addressing the common issue of smart home devices overwhelming consumer routers.
personal_relevance_summary: This article is highly relevant to your homelab and cybersecurity interests. It provides a practical, low-cost method to improve network performance and security by repurposing old hardware—a classic homelab technique. For Red Teaming, understanding network segmentation (LAN-to-WAN setup) is crucial for assessing lateral movement and isolation controls. The step-by-step configuration guide (IP assignment, DHCP disabling) is directly applicable to your DevOps and homelab projects. Additionally, the security benefits of isolating IoT devices align with your cybersecurity focus, offering a defensive technique you can both implement and test.
extra_advice: Consider using this setup as a testbed for Red Teaming exercises: set up a vulnerable IoT device on the secondary network and practice pivoting or escaping the subnet. Also, explore VLANs on your main router as a more advanced alternative to physical segmentation—this will deepen your networking skills for both homelab and professional engagements.
